Hypothesis testing: statistical inference

Convert the research hypothesis to null hypothesis and try to disprove. The decide which test & tails to be used.
decide on a significance level – the probability with which we are prepared to make a wrong decision if the null hypothesis is true.
Experimental design & do data collection

title: 9. Statistical Inference practices
text:
The NHANES data







Variable
Definition




id
A unique sample identifier


Gender
Gender (sex) of study participant coded as male or female


Age
Age in years at screening of study participant. Note: Subjects 80 years or older were recorded as 80.


Race
Reported race of study participant, including non-Hispanic Asian category: Mexican, Hispanic, White, Black, Asian, or Other. Not availale for 2009-10.


Education
Educational level of study participant Reported for participants aged 20 years or older. One of 8thGrade, 9-11thGrade, HighSchool, SomeCollege, or CollegeGrad.


MaritalStatus
Marital status of study participant. Reported for participants aged 20 years or older. One of Married, Widowed, Divorced, Separated, NeverMarried, or LivePartner (living with partner).


RelationshipStatus
Simplification of MaritalStatus, coded as Committed if MaritalStatus is Married or LivePartner, and Single otherwise.


Insured
Indicates whether the individual is covered by health insurance.


Income
Numerical version of HHIncome derived from the middle income in each category


Poverty
A ratio of family income to poverty guidelines. Smaller numbers indicate more poverty


HomeRooms
How many rooms are in home of study participant (counting kitchen but not bathroom). 13 rooms = 13 or more rooms.


HomeOwn
One of Home, Rent, or Other indicating whether the home of study participant or someone in their family is owned, rented or occupied by some other arrangement.


Work
Indicates whether the individual is current working or not.


Weight
Weight in kg


Height
Standing height in cm. Reported for participants aged 2 years or older.


BMI
Body mass index (weight/height2 in kg/m2). Reported for participants aged 2 years or older.


Pulse
60 second pulse rate


BPSys
Combined systolic blood pressure reading, following the procedure outlined for BPXSAR.


BPDia
Combined diastolic blood pressure reading, following the procedure outlined for BPXDAR.


Testosterone
Testerone total (ng/dL). Reported for participants aged 6 years or older. Not available for 2009-2010.


HDLChol
Direct HDL cholesterol in mmol/L. Reported for participants aged 6 years or older.


TotChol
Total HDL cholesterol in mmol/L. Reported for participants aged 6 years or older.


Diabetes
Study participant told by a doctor or health professional that they have diabetes. Reported for participants aged 1 year or older as Yes or No.


DiabetesAge
Age of study participant when first told they had diabetes. Reported for participants aged 1 year or older.


nPregnancies
How many times participant has been pregnant. Reported for female participants aged 20 years or older.


nBabies
How many of participants deliveries resulted in live births. Reported for female participants aged 20 years or older.


SleepHrsNight
Self-reported number of hours study participant usually gets at night on weekdays or workdays. Reported for participants aged 16 years and older.


PhysActive
Participant does moderate or vigorous-intensity sports, fitness or recreational activities (Yes or No). Reported for participants 12 years or older.


PhysActiveDays
Number of days in a typical week that participant does moderate or vigorous-intensity activity. Reported for participants 12 years or older.


AlcoholDay
Average number of drinks consumed on days that participant drank alcoholic beverages. Reported for participants aged 18 years or older.


AlcoholYear
Estimated number of days over the past year that participant drank alcoholic beverages. Reported for participants aged 18 years or older.


SmokingStatus
Smoking status: Current Former or Never.

title: 9. Statistical Inference practices
section: normality test: Shapiro-Wilk test of normality
text:
normality test: Shapiro-Wilk test of normality
 H_0 : data distribution = normal distribution   H_1 : data distribution \neq normal distribution 






Warning


W-statistic will be accurate but returned p-values may not be reliable if either of these apply:
Sample size is large (N > 2000) or small (N < 20) Too much data is censored (censored / N > 0.8)





Code
bmi_yes = @chain nh begin
    @drop_missing(BMI)
    @filter(Diabetes == "Yes")
    @pull(BMI)
end

HypothesisTests.ShapiroWilkTest(bmi_yes)


Shapiro-Wilk normality test
---------------------------
Population details:
    parameter of interest:   Squared correlation of data and expected order statistics of N(0,1) (W)
    value under h_0:         1.0
    point estimate:          0.966817

Test summary:
    outcome with 95% confidence: reject h_0
    one-sided p-value:           <1e-06

Details:
    number of observations: 366
    censored ratio:         0.0
    W-statistic:            0.966817

section: Levene Test of Homogeneity of Variances

Levene Test of Homogeneity of Variances
 H_0 : Var_{diabetes} = Var_{nondiabetes}   H_1 : Var_{diabetes} \neq Var_{nondiabetes}

    @drop_missing(BMI)

HypothesisTests.LeveneTest(bmi_diab_yes, bmi_diab_no)

title: 9. Statistical Inference practices
section: t-test for a population mean (variance unknown)
text:
t-test for a population mean (variance unknown)

Object

To investigate the significance of the difference between an assumed population mean μ0 and a sample mean x ̄.

Limitations

If the variance of the population σ 2 is known, a more powerful test is available: the Z-test for a population mean (Test 1).
The test is accurate if the population is normally distributed. If the population is not normal, the test will give an approximate guide.

Example: Most of diabetic patient are overweight

title: 9. Statistical Inference practices
text:
one sample T Test
 H_0 : \mu_{BMI_{adult,diabetic}} = 25   H_1 : \mu_{BMI_{adult,diabetic}} \neq 25 


Code
bmi_adults = @chain nh begin
    @drop_missing(BMI)
    @filter(Age >= 18)
    @pull(BMI)
end
HypothesisTests.OneSampleTTest(bmi_adults, 25)

-----------------

    parameter of interest:   Mean
    value under h_0:         25
    point estimate:          28.5457
    95% confidence interval: (28.33, 28.76)

Test summary:
    outcome with 95% confidence: reject h_0
    two-sided p-value:           <1e-99

Details:
    number of observations:   3676
    t-statistic:              32.795576685538244
    degrees of freedom:       3675
    empirical standard error: 0.10811441678103642

 H_0 : \mu_{BMI_{adult,diabetic}} \geq 25   H_1 : \mu_{BMI_{adult,diabetic}} < 25

section: one sample T Test: superiority test
text:
one sample T Test: superiority test

title: 9. Statistical Inference practices
section: t-test for two population means (variances unknown but equal)
text:
t-test for two population means (variances unknown but equal)

Object

To investigate the significance of the difference between the means of two populations.

Limitations

If the variance of the populations is known, a more powerful test is available: the Z-test for two population means (Test 2).
The test is accurate if the populations are normally distributed. If the populations are not normal, the test will give an approximate guide.

Object

To investigate the significance of the difference between the means of two populations.

Limitations

If the variances of the populations are known, a more powerful test is available: the Z-test for two population means (Test 3).

title: Data integrity
text:
Ethical on Data

“Fabrication and Falsification”

It is a primary responsibility of a researcher to avoid either a false statement or an omission that distorts the research record.
A researcher must not report anticipated research results that had not yet been observed at the time of submission of the report.

title: Data integrity
text:
Avoid misconduct on data

Data integrity
Use and mis-use of data
Ownership and access of data
Storage and Retention of Data
Data privacy

title: Data integrity
text:
Data integrity
maintain a clear and complete record of data acquired. sufficient detail to permit examination for the purpose of replicating the research, responding to questions that may result from unintentional error or misinterpretation, establishing authenticity of the records, and confirming the validity of the conclusions. destruction of research records or the failure to maintain and produce research records 1
http://www.provost.pitt.edu/documents/GUIDELINES%20FOR%20ETHICAL%20PRACTICES%20IN%20RESEARCH-FINALrevised2-March%202011.pdf

title: Data integrity
text:
Use and Mis-use

Negative result data
Large background Do not: withholding of information about confounding factors. If some data should be disregarded for a stated reason, confirmed by an approved statistical test for neglecting outliers, the reason should be stated in the published accounts.

Any intentional or reckless disregard for the truth in reporting observations may be considered to be an act of research misconduct.

title: Data integrity

Survey question:
How often do you go grocery?

Every day
Once a week
Biweekly
Once a month
Less than once a month
Never

Inaccurate number from instruments

title: Data integrity
text:
Example: Incomplete data
Missing data
Handling: - Prevent - Listwise or case deletion, Pairwise deletion - Imputation: Mean substitution, Regression imputation, Maximum likelihood, Expectation-Maximization - Sensitivity analysis - Last observation carried forward

title: Data integrity
text:
Example: Inconsistent data
In your survey about demographic variables, including age, ethnicity, education level, and socioeconomic status.
One participant enters “13” for their age PhD-level education as their highest attained degree
Or
“14” for their age Have 2 children

title: Data integrity
text:
Example: Duplicate entries
Fills out the questionnaire twice Record twice or multiple time Subject duplication

title: Data integrity
text:
Example: Nonuniform data
In a survey, ask to enter their gross salary in U.S. dollars. Some respond with their monthly salary others report their annual salary Using difference units

title: Data integrity
text:
Ownership and access of data
Research data obtained in studies performed at the University and/or by employees of the University are not the property of the researcher who generated or observed them or even of the principal investigator of the research group. They belong to the University, which can be held accountable for the integrity of the data even if the researchers have left the University.

Stored securely for at least five years after completion

title: Machine Learning
text:
What is Artificial Intelligence?
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the simulation of human intelligence processes by machines, especially computer systems.


Core Capabilities

🧠 Reasoning — logical problem solving
📚 Learning — improving from experience
👁️ Perception — interpreting sensory input
💬 Natural Language — understanding text/speech
🤖 Planning — goal-directed action


Application Domains

Drug discovery & design
Medical image analysis
Clinical decision support
Genomics & proteomics
Pharmacovigilance

title: Machine Learning
text:
The AI Hierarchy




AI: Artificial Intelligence  the science and engineering of making intelligent machines, especially intelligent computer programs





ML: Machine Learning  design and train algorithms to learn from and act on data





DL: Deep Learning  multi-layered neural networks, called deep neural networks, to simulate the complex decision-making power of the human brain





GenAI: Generative Deep Learning  a subset of AI used to produce text, images, videos, or others response to specific prompts. 1

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generative_artificial_intelligence

title: Machine Learning
text:

Feature
Traditional AI
Machine Learning




Knowledge
Hard-coded rules
Learned from data


Adaptability
Manual updates needed
Improves automatically


Scalability
Limited
Scales with data


Interpretability
High (explicit rules)
Often lower (black box)


Example
Expert systems, chess engines
Spam filters, image classifiers

title: Machine Learning
text:
What is Machine Learning?

Machine Learning (ML) is a field of AI that enables systems to learn and improve from experience without being explicitly programmed.

Instead of writing rules:
IF temperature > 38.5 THEN fever = True
IF fever AND cough THEN risk = High
...
We let the algorithm discover patterns from labeled examples.

title: Machine Learning
text:
Machine Learning Key Components

Data — examples & labels
Model — mathematical function
Loss function — how wrong are we?
Optimization — minimize loss

title: Machine Learning
text:
Types of Machine Learning


Supervised Learning

Labeled training data
Learn input → output mapping
Examples:

Classification
Regression

Applications: diagnosis, prognosis


Unsupervised Learning

No labels required
Find hidden structure
Examples:

Clustering
Dimensionality reduction

Applications: patient stratification


Reinforcement Learning

Agent + environment
Learn by reward/penalty
Examples:

Game playing
Drug dosing optimization

Applications: clinical decision

title: Machine Learning
text:
Key Concepts: Bias-Variance Tradeoff



Underfitting (High Bias)

Model too simple

Poor on training AND test data
Fix: more complex model, more features

Overfitting (High Variance)

Model too complex
Great on training, poor on test
Fix: regularization, more data, simpler model

Sweet Spot

Balances bias & variance
Generalizes well to new data








xychart-beta
  title "Bias-Variance Tradeoff"
  x-axis ["Simple", "Low", "Medium", "High", "Complex"]
  y-axis "Error" 0 --> 10
  line [9, 6, 3.5, 2.5, 2]
  line [9, 5, 3.5, 5, 8]







📉 Train Error (solid) decreases with complexity
📈 Test Error (line) — U-shaped — optimal at middle
⬆️ Left = High Bias (Underfitting)
⬆️ Right = High Variance (Overfitting)

title: Machine Learning
text:
What is Deep Learning?
Deep Learning (DL) uses artificial neural networks with many layers to learn hierarchical representations from raw data.


Why “Deep”?

Multiple hidden layers
Each layer learns more abstract features
Pixels → Edges → Shapes → Objects

Key Advantage

Automatic feature extraction — no manual feature engineering needed
Excels at images, text, audio, sequences


Input Layer   Hidden Layers   Output Layer
   [x₁] ──┐
   [x₂] ──┤─→[●][●][●]─→[●][●]─→[ŷ]
   [x₃] ──┤─→[●][●][●]─→[●][●]
   [x₄] ──┘
   
   Each ● = neuron (weighted sum + activation)

title: Machine Learning
text:
Neural Network Components

Neuron (Perceptron): z = \sum_{i} w_i x_i + b, \quad \hat{y} = \sigma(z)
Activation Functions:



Function
Formula
Use Case




Sigmoid
1/(1+e⁻ᶻ)
Binary output


ReLU
max(0, z)
Hidden layers (CNN)


Softmax
eᶻⁱ/Σeᶻʲ
Multi-class output


Tanh
(eᶻ-e⁻ᶻ)/(eᶻ+e⁻ᶻ)
RNN hidden states



Training: Backpropagation + Gradient Descent
w \leftarrow w - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w}

text:
Setting Up the Environment
# Install required packages (run once)
using Pkg
Pkg.add([
    "MLJ",
    "MLJDecisionTreeInterface",  # Decision Trees
    "XGBoost",                   # Gradient Boosting
    "CSV",                       # Read CSV files
    "DataFrames",                # Data manipulation
    "Plots",                     # Visualization
    "StatsPlots",                # Statistical plots
    "CategoricalArrays"          # Categorical variables
])
# Load packages
using MLJ, CSV, DataFrames, CategoricalArrays
using Plots, StatsPlots






Tip


Julia packages are compiled on first use — subsequent runs are much faster.

title: Machine Learning
text:
MLJ.jl: The Unified ML Framework
MLJ (Machine Learning in Julia) provides a unified interface to 150+ ML algorithms.
using MLJ

# Load a model
Tree = @load DecisionTreeClassifier pkg=DecisionTree

# Instantiate with hyperparameters
tree = Tree(max_depth=5)

# Wrap data in MLJ format
X, y = ... # features and target
mach = machine(tree, X, y)

# Train
fit!(mach, rows=train)

# Predict
ŷ = predict(mach, rows=test)

# Evaluate
evaluate!(mach, resampling=CV(nfolds=5), measure=accuracy)

title: Machine Learning
text:
The Titanic Dataset
Kaggle Titanic Dataset download
Objective: Predict whether a passenger survived the Titanic disaster.
This is a Binary Classification Problem: - Target: Survived (0 = No, 1 = Yes)




Feature
Type
Description




Pclass
Ordinal
Passenger class (1, 2, 3)


Sex
Categorical
male / female


Age
Continuous
Age in years


SibSp
Integer
# siblings/spouses aboard


Parch
Integer
# parents/children aboard


Fare
Continuous
Ticket fare


Embarked
Categorical
Port (C, Q, S)

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 1: Load and Inspect Data
using CSV, DataFrames

# Load data
df = CSV.read("titanic.csv", DataFrame)

# Inspect
println("Shape: ", size(df))
println("\nFirst 5 rows:")
first(df, 5)
# Basic statistics
describe(df, :all)
# Check missing values
missing_count = combine(df, names(df) .=> (x -> sum(ismissing.(x))) .=> names(df))
println(missing_count)






Note


The Titanic dataset has missing values in Age (~20%), Cabin (~77%), and Embarked (~0.2%).

text:
Step 2: Exploratory Data Analysis
using StatsPlots

# Survival by gender
@df df groupedbar(:Sex, :Survived, 
    group=:Survived,
    xlabel="Sex", ylabel="Count",
    title="Survival by Gender",
    labels=["Died" "Survived"],
    color=[:salmon :steelblue])
# Age distribution by survival
@df df violin(:Survived, :Age, 
    group=:Survived,
    xlabel="Survived", ylabel="Age",
    title="Age Distribution by Survival",
    labels=["Died" "Survived"])
# Survival rate by passenger class
survival_by_class = combine(groupby(df, :Pclass),
    :Survived => mean => :survival_rate)
println(survival_by_class)

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 3: Data Preprocessing
using DataFrames, CategoricalArrays, Statistics

df_clean = copy(df)

# 1. Drop irrelevant columns
select!(df_clean, Not([:PassengerId, :Name, :Ticket, :Cabin]))

# 2. Impute missing Age with median
age_median = median(skipmissing(df_clean.Age))
df_clean.Age = coalesce.(df_clean.Age, age_median)

# 3. Impute missing Embarked with mode
df_clean.Embarked = coalesce.(df_clean.Embarked, "S")

# 4. Encode Sex: female=1, male=0
df_clean.Sex = ifelse.(df_clean.Sex .== "female", 1, 0)

# 5. One-hot encode Embarked
df_clean.Embarked_C = Int.(df_clean.Embarked .== "C")
df_clean.Embarked_Q = Int.(df_clean.Embarked .== "Q")
select!(df_clean, Not(:Embarked))

# 6. Feature engineering
df_clean.FamilySize = df_clean.SibSp .+ df_clean.Parch .+ 1
df_clean.IsAlone = Int.(df_clean.FamilySize .== 1)

println("Processed shape: ", size(df_clean))

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 4: Prepare MLJ-Compatible Data
using MLJ

# Separate features and target
target = :Survived
features = setdiff(names(df_clean), [string(target)])

X = select(df_clean, features)
y = df_clean[:, target]

# Convert target to categorical (required for classification)
y = CategoricalArray(y)

# Coerce feature types for MLJ
X = coerce(X, 
    :Pclass     => OrderedFactor,
    :Sex        => Count,
    :Age        => Continuous,
    :SibSp      => Count,
    :Parch      => Count,
    :Fare       => Continuous,
    :FamilySize => Count,
    :IsAlone    => Count
)

println("X:", schema(X))
println("y:", scitype(y))

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 5: Train/Test Split
using MLJ

# Reproducible split: 80% train, 20% test
train, test = partition(eachindex(y), 0.8, shuffle=true, rng=42)

println("Training samples: ", length(train))
println("Test samples:     ", length(test))
println("Survival rate (train): ", 
    round(mean(y[train] .== "1") * 100, digits=1), "%")
println("Survival rate (test):  ", 
    round(mean(y[test] .== "1") * 100, digits=1), "%")

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 6: Train a Decision Tree
using MLJ
MLJ.@load DecisionTreeClassifier pkg=DecisionTree

# Define model
tree_clf = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5)

# Wrap in machine (binds model + data)
mach_tree = machine(tree_clf, X, y)

# Train on training set
fit!(mach_tree, rows=train, verbosity=0)

# Predict on test set
ŷ_tree = predict_mode(mach_tree, rows=test)

# Evaluate
acc_tree = accuracy(ŷ_tree, y[test])
println("Decision Tree Accuracy: ", round(acc_tree * 100, digits=2), "%")

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 7: Train a Random Forest
MLJ.@load RandomForestClassifier pkg=DecisionTree

# Define model with hyperparameters
rf_clf = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_trees   = 100,
    max_depth = 10,
    min_samples_leaf = 5,
    n_subfeatures = 3
)

mach_rf = machine(rf_clf, X, y)
fit!(mach_rf, rows=train, verbosity=0)
ŷ_rf = predict_mode(mach_rf, rows=test)

acc_rf = accuracy(ŷ_rf, y[test])
println("Random Forest Accuracy: ", round(acc_rf * 100, digits=2), "%")

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 8: Cross-Validation Evaluation
using MLJ

# 5-fold cross-validation
cv = CV(nfolds=5, shuffle=true, rng=42)

# Evaluate multiple metrics at once
eval_rf = evaluate(rf_clf, X, y,
    resampling = cv,
    measures   = [accuracy, f1score, auc],
    verbosity  = 0
)

println("\n=== Random Forest Cross-Validation ===")
for (m, v) in zip(eval_rf.measure, eval_rf.measurement)
    println("$(m):  ", round(v, digits=4))
end
println("Per-fold accuracy: ", round.(eval_rf.per_fold[1], digits=3))

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 9: Hyperparameter Tuning
using MLJ

# Define search range
r1 = range(rf_clf, :n_trees,      lower=50,  upper=300, scale=:linear)
r2 = range(rf_clf, :max_depth,    lower=3,   upper=15)
r3 = range(rf_clf, :n_subfeatures, lower=2,  upper=6)

# Grid search with cross-validation
tuned_rf = TunedModel(
    model     = rf_clf,
    resampling = CV(nfolds=5, rng=42),
    tuning    = Grid(resolution=5),
    range     = [r1, r2, r3],
    measure   = accuracy
)

mach_tuned = machine(tuned_rf, X, y)
fit!(mach_tuned, rows=train, verbosity=0)

# Best model
best_model = fitted_params(mach_tuned).best_model
println("Best parameters:")
println("  n_trees:       ", best_model.n_trees)
println("  max_depth:     ", best_model.max_depth)
println("  n_subfeatures: ", best_model.n_subfeatures)

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 10: Feature Importance
# Get feature importances from trained Random Forest
fp = fitted_params(mach_rf)
importances = fp.forest.feature_importances

# Sort and display
feat_names = names(X)
imp_df = sort(
    DataFrame(feature=feat_names, importance=importances),
    :importance, rev=true
)
println(imp_df)

# Plot
bar(imp_df.feature, imp_df.importance,
    xlabel="Feature",
    ylabel="Importance",
    title="Random Forest Feature Importance (Titanic)",
    xrotation=30,
    legend=false,
    color=:steelblue)

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 11: Model Comparison
using MLJ

# Load all model types
MLJ.@load LogisticClassifier pkg=MLJLinearModels
MLJ.@load XGBoostClassifier  pkg=XGBoost

models = [
    ("Logistic Regression", LogisticClassifier()),
    ("Decision Tree",       DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5)),
    ("Random Forest",       RandomForestClassifier(n_trees=100)),
    ("XGBoost",             XGBoostClassifier(num_round=100)),
]

results = []
for (name, model) in models
    ev = evaluate(model, X, y,
        resampling = CV(nfolds=5, rng=42),
        measures   = [accuracy, f1score],
        verbosity  = 0)
    push!(results, (
        Model    = name,
        Accuracy = round(ev.measurement[1], digits=4),
        F1_Score = round(ev.measurement[2], digits=4)
    ))
end

println(DataFrame(results))

title: Machine Learning
text:
Model Comparison Results
Model                  Accuracy   F1_Score
─────────────────────────────────────────
Logistic Regression    0.8045     0.7412
Decision Tree          0.7921     0.7283
Random Forest          0.8247     0.7698
XGBoost                0.8316     0.7801
# Visualize comparison
model_names = [r.Model for r in results]
accuracies  = [r.Accuracy for r in results]

bar(model_names, accuracies,
    title="Model Accuracy Comparison — Titanic",
    xlabel="Model", ylabel="Accuracy (5-fold CV)",
    ylim=(0.7, 0.9), legend=false,
    color=[:steelblue, :salmon, :seagreen, :orchid],
    xrotation=20)

title: Machine Learning
text:
Step 12: Confusion Matrix & ROC Curve
using MLJ, Plots

# Predictions with probabilities
ŷ_prob = predict(mach_rf, rows=test)

# Confusion matrix
cm = ConfusionMatrix(zero_division=0)
println("Confusion Matrix:")
println(confusion_matrix(predict_mode(mach_rf, rows=test), y[test]))

# ROC Curve
fprs, tprs, thresholds = roc_curve(ŷ_prob, y[test])
auc_val = auc(ŷ_prob, y[test])

plot(fprs, tprs,
    xlabel="False Positive Rate",
    ylabel="True Positive Rate",
    title="ROC Curve — Random Forest (Titanic)",
    label="AUC = $(round(auc_val, digits=3))",
    linewidth=2, color=:steelblue)
plot!([0,1], [0,1], linestyle=:dash, color=:gray, label="Random")

title: Machine Learning
text:
Complete Pipeline: Summary
using MLJ, CSV, DataFrames, CategoricalArrays

# ── 1. Load
df = CSV.read("titanic.csv", DataFrame)

# ── 2. Preprocess (function defined earlier)
df_clean = preprocess_titanic(df)

# ── 3. Feature/target split
X = select(df_clean, Not(:Survived))
y = CategoricalArray(df_clean.Survived)
X = coerce(X, :Pclass => OrderedFactor, :Age => Continuous, :Fare => Continuous)

# ── 4. Split
train, test = partition(eachindex(y), 0.8, shuffle=true, rng=42)

# ── 5. Train best model
MLJ.@load RandomForestClassifier pkg=DecisionTree
model = RandomForestClassifier(n_trees=100, max_depth=10)
mach  = machine(model, X, y)
fit!(mach, rows=train)

# ── 6. Evaluate
ŷ = predict_mode(mach, rows=test)
println("Final Accuracy: ", round(accuracy(ŷ, y[test]) * 100, digits=2), "%")
